summary: The lateral ventricles communicate with the 3rd ventricle via the interventricular foramen (of Monro).
The lateral ventricles communicate directly with the 4th ventricle.
The lateral ventricles communicate with the 3rd ventricle via the cerebral aqueduct.
CSF is produced by the choroid plexus and absorbed by arachnoid granulations into the superior sagittal sinus.
CSF is produced by the ependymal lining and absorbed by the choroid plexus.
CSF is produced in the subarachnoid space and absorbed by the pia mater.
In aqueductal stenosis, the lateral and 3rd ventricles dilate but the 4th ventricle remains normal.
In aqueductal stenosis, only the 4th ventricle dilates.
In aqueductal stenosis, all four ventricles dilate equally.
At the optic chiasm, nasal retinal fibres cross while temporal fibres remain ipsilateral.
At the optic chiasm, temporal fibres cross while nasal fibres remain ipsilateral.
All fibres cross at the optic chiasm.
A pituitary tumour compressing the optic chiasm causes bitemporal hemianopia.
A pituitary tumour causes homonymous hemianopia.
A pituitary tumour causes total blindness in one eye only.
The visual pathway relays in the lateral geniculate body of the thalamus before reaching the occipital cortex.
The visual pathway relays in the medial geniculate body of the thalamus.
The visual pathway bypasses the thalamus entirely.
CSF exits the 4th ventricle through the median aperture (Magendie) and two lateral apertures (Luschka).
CSF exits the 4th ventricle through a single midline foramen.
CSF exits the 4th ventricle through the cerebral aqueduct.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the ventricular system and visual pathway.
Good effort! Revise CSF circulation and field defects.
